hook: Brick church with a flat wall-belfry on the spot the bull stopped
facts: Marks the place where, legend says, the bull dragged the martyr Saturnin (Sernin) until the rope broke in the 3rd century
Distinctive 14th-century clocher-mur, a flat gabled wall pierced for bells instead of a tower
Built of the local pink brick on the rue du Taur ('Street of the Bull') between the Capitole and Saint-Sernin
Eugene Puget organ of 1880; listed historical monument since 1987
legend: Legend says the church rose exactly where Saturnin's body came free of the maddened bull that dragged him to his death
w: Pagan priests, the story goes, lashed bishop Saturnin to a bull and let it loose; the rope snapped here, and a church rose on the spot. No tower, just a flat brick wall pierced for its bells, a clocher-mur facing the street that still carries the beast's name. The bull won the street; the saint won the wall.

hook: Riverside basilica of the Black Virgin, patron of women in labour
facts: Name derives from 'Deaurata', for the golden mosaics of an early church on the site of a temple to Apollo
Houses a Black Virgin venerated by pregnant women; priests still bless birthing belts here
The first replacement statue was burned by revolutionaries on the Place du Capitole in 1799; today's icon dates from 1807
The old monastery became a tobacco factory after the Revolution
legend: They say the Black Virgin protects the city and brings safe delivery to expectant mothers, drawing pilgrims for centuries
w: The name remembers golden mosaics long gone, 'deaurata', set in a church built over a temple to Apollo. Pilgrims came not for gold but for the Black Virgin, protector of women in labour; priests still bless birthing belts at her altar. Revolutionaries burned one statue on the Place du Capitole; the copy keeps her vigil over the Garonne.

hook: Pink-brick church crowned by a glittering 1878 ceramic tympanum
facts: Name from Latin 'dealbata', whitewashed, for the limewashed white walls of the earlier church
The polychrome ceramic tympanum, after Fra Angelico's Coronation of the Virgin, was made by Gaston Virebent in 1878
Its bell tower, at 91 metres the tallest in the city, collapsed dramatically in 1926
Rebuilt around 1480 after a fire; in the Carmes quarter
w: Once limewashed white, hence 'Dalbade', now plain pink brick, but for one extravagance: a ceramic tympanum blazing gold and blue above the door, fired by Gaston Virebent in 1878 after Fra Angelico. The 91-metre tower, tallest in Toulouse, simply fell down one day in 1926. The brick endured; the glaze still glitters.

hook: Roman marbles in a 16th-century college built over a necropolis
facts: Holds France's second-largest collection of Roman antiquities after the Louvre
Centrepiece: the imperial busts and sculptures from the Roman villa of Chiragan
Housed in a former university college rebuilt in 1523 for poor students; opened as a museum in 1892
The basement preserves an early Christian necropolis and a 5th-6th century lime kiln, near Saturnin's tomb
w: A college for poor scholars in 1523, raised over graveyard ground, hospital and barracks by turns, now keeping France's second-greatest hoard of Roman marble after the Louvre. The Chiragan villa gave up its emperors in stone, busts by the rank. Dig into the cellar and you reach an early Christian necropolis and a lime kiln. Layers all the way down.

hook: Renaissance mansion fronted by strange, lifelike caryatids and atlantes
facts: Built from 1516 for the lawyer and capitoul Beringuier Maynier; nicknamed for a nearby tavern's bunch-of-grapes sign
Bachelier carved the windowed facades with caryatids and atlantes from around 1547
Ground-floor figures show hybrid bodies with lion paws; upper figures strain under the entablature
A spiral stair tower carries medallion busts; corner site on rue du Languedoc and rue Ozenne
w: Toulouse made fortunes from blue woad dye and poured them into mansions like this one, begun in 1516 and named, prosaically, for a tavern's grape sign next door. Bachelier carved its windows with stone figures who seem to suffer: men straining under the cornice, lower bodies fading into lion paws and pilasters. Wealth turned to anatomy lesson.

hook: 1841 hilltop observatory still run by amateur stargazers
facts: Built in 1841 by Frederic Petit atop the Calvinet (Jolimont) hill, after three years of haggling with the city
Main building and three domes by architect Urbain Vitry; listed historical monument since 1987
Rises about 50 metres above the city, sited to clear the Garonne mists
Now home to the Societe d'Astronomie Populaire, which opens it to the public
w: It took three years of arguing with the council before they laid a stone on the Jolimont hill in 1841, high enough to clear the river fog. Frederic Petit got his observatory; Urbain Vitry gave it a main block and three white domes. The professionals moved to a campus in 1981, but amateur astronomers kept the hilltop and still crank the domes open at night.

hook: A pocket of Kyoto behind the business district, mayor's pet project
facts: Modelled on Kyoto's Katsura villa; inaugurated in 1981 for 20 years of Toulouse-Kyoto twinning
Built at the wish of mayor Pierre Baudis, who loved such gardens abroad, especially Dublin's
Occupies 7,000 square metres inside the larger Compans-Caffarelli park
Features a tea pavilion, a red bridge, a dry garden with crane and turtle islands, and a Mount Fuji mound
w: A mayor with a taste for raked gravel, Pierre Baudis fell for Japanese gardens on his travels, the one in Dublin most of all, and had the city build him this one in 1981 to mark the Kyoto twinning. Behind glass towers sit a tea pavilion, a vermilion bridge, crane and turtle islands and a small Mount Fuji. Kyoto by way of Dublin, in pink-brick Toulouse.

hook: Brick keep built to guard gunpowder and the city's freedoms
facts: Built in 1525 behind the Capitole to store gunpowder and the municipal archives
Its defensive look earned it the nickname 'Donjon' (keep); also called the Tour des Archives
Charter chest once needed eight keys, one held by each capitoul, to open
Restored by Viollet-le-Duc and crowned with a Flemish-style belfry; tourist office since 1948
w: Behind the Capitole stands a stout brick tower that never fought a siege. Raised in 1525, it guarded gunpowder and, more jealously, the city's charters, locked in a chest that opened only with eight keys, one per ruling capitoul. Viollet-le-Duc tidied it up and added a Flemish spire. The keep of municipal liberty now hands out tourist maps.

n: Monument aux combattants de la Haute-Garonne
hook: War memorial whose nude Victory caused a decade of scandal
facts: A 1,000-tonne arch of eight columns, 15 metres high, designed by architect Leon Jaussely; inaugurated 1928
Camille Raynaud's relief shows Victory naked but for a poilu's helmet, scandalising the committee
The dispute over replacing it reached the Conseil d'Etat, which let her stay in 1935
Listed historical monument since 2018
w: Most war memorials offer a draped, severe Victory. Camille Raynaud carved a plump, naked one wearing nothing but a soldier's helmet, and the committee was horrified. The row dragged to the Conseil d'Etat, which in 1935 ruled she could stay, partly because recutting a thousand tonnes of stone cost too much. Decency lost to the budget.
